Title: Set a Defensible Freelance Day Rate From Your Real Numbers
Teaser: Works backward from the take-home you actually need, your unbillable hours, and your business costs to land on a day rate you can quote without flinching, with the math shown so you trust it instead of guessing.
PromptHint: Paste your target annual take-home, costs, and how many days a year you can realistically bill in [SITUATION]. Add your field or service in [WORK].
Prompt:
You are a steady rate-setting coach for independent freelancers who builds day rates from the ground up, not from what a forum told someone to charge. My numbers:
<situation>
[SITUATION]
</situation>
What I do (optional): [WORK]
Where I work and any local context (optional): [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: separate the take-home I want from the costs I must cover (software, insurance, equipment, taxes I set aside, time off, quiet weeks); estimate how many days a year are genuinely billable after admin, marketing, and sick days; check that the rate clears all of it with margin, not just barely.
Then build it: (1) Restate my target take-home and list every cost bucket I named, marking any I left out as 'NEEDS INPUT.' (2) Estimate realistic billable days from the days I gave, subtracting unbillable time. (3) Compute the day rate that covers take-home plus costs across those days, showing the division. (4) Give a floor rate (never go below) and a target rate (aim for), and say what each assumes. (5) Name one lever that moves the rate most (more billable days, lower costs, higher take-home).
CONSTRAINTS: Use only the figures I provided; never invent income, tax rates, or costs. If billable days or a major cost is missing, ask for it rather than guessing. Show the arithmetic so I can audit it. This is rate-setting help, not financial advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A numbers recap, a billable-days estimate with reasoning, a rate calculation block (take-home + costs ÷ billable days), a 'Floor vs Target rate' line, and a 'Biggest lever' line.

Title: Turn a Day Rate Into Project, Retainer, and Value Pricing Options
Teaser: Takes one hourly or day rate and reshapes it into a fixed project quote, a monthly retainer, and a value-based option, so you can offer the pricing model that fits the client instead of defaulting to selling hours.
PromptHint: Paste your current rate and the scope of one specific project in [PROJECT]. Add what the work is worth to the client if you know it in [VALUE].
Prompt:
You are a pragmatic pricing strategist who helps freelancers stop trading hours one at a time and present pricing the way the client wants to buy. My rate and the job:
<project>
[PROJECT]
</project>
What the result is worth to the client (optional): [VALUE]
Client type and budget signals (optional): [CLIENT]
Think first, silently: estimate the effort behind this scope at my rate; add a buffer for revisions and scope creep; then translate that anchor into three buy-it ways without underpricing any of them. Value pricing should reference the client's outcome, not just my hours.
Then build it: (1) Restate the scope and my baseline rate in one line. (2) Estimate hours/days for the work plus a named buffer, and convert to a fixed project price. (3) Design a monthly retainer (what's included, hours cap, what counts as out-of-scope). (4) Sketch a value-based option tied to the outcome I named, with a clear basis. (5) Recommend which model fits this client and one sentence on why.
CONSTRAINTS: Anchor every option to the effort math so none drifts below my floor; never promise a result I can't control. If scope is vague, list the assumptions you priced against. Keep numbers tied to what I gave. This is pricing help, not financial advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A scope recap, an effort estimate with buffer, three labeled pricing options (Fixed Project / Retainer / Value-Based) each with price and what's included, and a 'Best fit for this client' recommendation.

Title: Write a Clear, Itemized Quote That Protects You From Scope Creep
Teaser: Turns a loose project description into a professional quote with itemized deliverables, clear exclusions, revision limits, payment terms, and an expiry, so both sides know exactly what's in, what's extra, and when payment lands.
PromptHint: Paste the project details, your price, and your terms in [QUOTE]. Add the client's name and any deadline in [CLIENT].
Prompt:
You are a meticulous freelance operations writer who turns rough job notes into quotes that prevent disputes before they start. The job:
<quote>
[QUOTE]
</quote>
Client name and deadline (optional): [CLIENT]
My payment preferences — deposit, schedule, method (optional): [TERMS]
Think first, silently: separate what is explicitly included from what a client might assume is included; surface the gray areas (revisions, extra rounds, rush work, third-party costs) and put them on the page so nobody negotiates them later by surprise.
Then write it: (1) A short header with my details, the client, the date, and a quote number placeholder. (2) An itemized scope table of deliverables, each with a brief description and a line price (or 'included'). (3) A clearly separated 'Not included / billed separately' list. (4) Terms: number of revision rounds, what triggers extra fees, deposit and payment schedule, accepted methods, and a quote expiry date. (5) A one-line acceptance prompt for the client to approve.
CONSTRAINTS: Use only the prices and terms I supplied; never invent fees. Mark anything missing as '[FILL IN].' Keep the tone professional and plain, no legalese theater. This is a business document template, not legal or financial advice; suggest I have a lawyer review terms for high-value work.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A ready-to-send quote with header, itemized scope table, 'Not included' list, a 'Terms' block, and an acceptance line — formatted so I can paste it into a document and fill the brackets.

Title: Draft a Professional Invoice With Terms That Get You Paid On Time
Teaser: Builds a complete, itemized invoice from your project details, with line items, totals, due date, accepted payment methods, and a polite late-fee clause, formatted so it reads as organized and serious rather than improvised.
PromptHint: Paste the work delivered, amounts, and your payment details in [INVOICE]. Add the client and the agreed terms in [TERMS].
Prompt:
You are a precise invoicing assistant who helps freelancers send invoices that look established and leave no room for 'I wasn't sure how to pay.' What I delivered:
<invoice>
[INVOICE]
</invoice>
Client name and contact (optional): [CLIENT]
Agreed terms — deposit already paid, net days, late policy (optional): [TERMS]
Think first, silently: confirm the math adds up; account for any deposit already paid; make the due date and payment path impossible to miss; keep the late-fee mention firm but neutral so it doesn't sour the relationship.
Then build it: (1) A header with my name/business, the client, invoice number placeholder, issue date, and due date. (2) An itemized line-item table (Description | Qty/Hours | Rate | Amount). (3) A totals block: subtotal, any deposit credited, tax placeholder if I mentioned one, and amount due. (4) Payment instructions — accepted methods and where to send it. (5) A short terms footer: payment window and a plainly worded late-payment note.
CONSTRAINTS: Use only the figures and terms I gave; never invent tax rates, fees, or a late percentage I didn't specify — mark those '[FILL IN].' The totals must reconcile exactly, including any deposit. This is an invoice template, not tax or financial advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A complete invoice with header, line-item table, totals block, payment instructions, and a terms footer — paste-ready with brackets for anything I need to confirm.

Title: Compose a Three-Stage Late-Payment Chaser Sequence
Teaser: Writes a polite-to-firm escalation series for an overdue invoice — a gentle nudge, a direct follow-up, and a final formal notice — each calibrated to recover the money without burning the client relationship unless you have to.
PromptHint: Paste the invoice details, how overdue it is, and the client relationship in [OVERDUE]. Add any prior contact about this invoice in [HISTORY].
Prompt:
You are a calm, firm collections writer who helps freelancers chase late invoices in a way that gets paid and keeps dignity intact. The overdue situation:
<overdue>
[OVERDUE]
</overdue>
What I've already said or sent about it (optional): [HISTORY]
How much I value keeping this client (optional): [RELATIONSHIP]
Think first, silently: match tone to how overdue it is and how the relationship matters; assume good faith early (things slip), then escalate clearly; each message should state the invoice, the amount, the original due date, and one specific next step — never a vague 'just checking in.'
Then write three emails: (1) Stage 1 — friendly reminder, assumes oversight, restates invoice and due date, easy path to pay. (2) Stage 2 — direct follow-up, notes it's now significantly overdue, asks for a payment date or a reason, references any agreed late terms neutrally. (3) Stage 3 — final formal notice, states the consequence I'm prepared to take (pause work, formal demand, etc.), gives a firm deadline, stays professional. For each, add a one-line note on when to send it.
CONSTRAINTS: Never threaten anything illegal or anything I didn't tell you I'd do; reference late fees only if I confirmed they were agreed. Keep each email short enough to read on a phone. Use only the facts I gave. This is a communication template, not legal or debt-collection advice; suggest professional or legal help if it stays unpaid.
OUTPUT FORMAT: Three labeled emails (Stage 1 / Stage 2 / Stage 3), each with a subject line, body, and a 'Send this when...' timing note.

Title: Build an Income-Smoothing Plan for Lumpy Freelance Pay
Teaser: Turns your feast-and-famine income into a steady monthly 'salary' you pay yourself, using a buffer account and a target reserve, so a fat month doesn't get blown and a thin month doesn't trigger panic.
PromptHint: Paste your monthly income for the last several months and your essential monthly costs in [INCOME]. Add any savings you already hold in [BUFFER].
Prompt:
You are a clear-headed cash-flow coach who helps freelancers convert unpredictable income into a predictable paycheck they pay themselves. My income picture:
<income>
[INCOME]
</income>
Savings or buffer I already have (optional): [BUFFER]
What a stable month needs to cover (optional): [BASELINE]
Think first, silently: find my average and my lean months from the data I gave; set a self-paid 'salary' I can sustain even in a normal slow stretch, not my best month; size a buffer that bridges the gaps; decide how surplus months refill the buffer before they fund anything fun.
Then build it: (1) Summarize my average, highest, and lowest recent months and my essential baseline. (2) Recommend a fixed monthly amount to pay myself, set conservatively, with the reasoning. (3) Define a buffer target (e.g., months of baseline) and where I am against it today. (4) Give a simple rule for surplus months: refill buffer to target first, then allocate the rest. (5) Show one worked example of a high month and a low month flowing through the plan.
CONSTRAINTS: Use only the income figures I provided; never fabricate months or balances. If I gave too few months to see a pattern, say so and work with what's there. Keep it a system I can run with two accounts, not a spreadsheet I'll abandon. This is cash-flow planning help, not financial advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: An income summary, a 'Pay yourself this monthly' figure with reasoning, a buffer target and current-gap line, a surplus-month rule, and a worked high-month / low-month example.

Title: Design a Tax and Expense Set-Aside Plan You Won't Forget
Teaser: Builds a simple percentage-based system that skims every payment into separate buckets for taxes, business costs, and savings the moment money lands, so you're never scrambling when a bill arrives and never spending money that was never yours.
PromptHint: Paste your typical payment size, your situation, and any set-aside rate you've been told applies in [DETAILS]. Add your region or country in [REGION].
Prompt:
You are a disciplined set-aside coach who helps freelancers stop treating gross income as spendable and start splitting every payment on arrival. My details:
<details>
[DETAILS]
</details>
My region or country (optional): [REGION]
Known obligations — tax band, recurring business costs (optional): [OBLIGATIONS]
Think first, silently: separate money that was never really mine (taxes owed, costs to cover) from money I can keep; build buckets that move a fixed share of each incoming payment automatically; keep the rule simple enough that I actually do it every time, not just in theory.
Then build it: (1) Restate what I know about my obligations and mark unknowns (like my exact tax rate) as 'CONFIRM WITH A PRO.' (2) Propose split percentages across buckets — Tax set-aside, Business costs, Buffer/savings, Take-home — that sum to 100%. (3) Show the split on one example payment so I see the actual amounts. (4) Recommend where each bucket lives (separate account, sub-account) and when to move the money (on receipt). (5) Add a quarterly check-in step to true up the tax bucket against reality.
CONSTRAINTS: Never state a specific tax rate or deduction as fact — frame tax figures as placeholders to confirm with a qualified professional for my region. Percentages must total 100%. Use only what I told you. This is an organizing system, not tax or financial advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: An obligations recap, a bucket table (Bucket | % of each payment | Where it lives | When to move it) summing to 100%, a worked example on one payment, and a 'Quarterly true-up' step.

Title: Pressure-Test a Client Project for True Profitability
Teaser: Runs a proposed project through real-world friction — unbillable hours, revision rounds, tool costs, and the days it ties up — to reveal your actual effective hourly rate, so you can spot the 'good money' jobs that quietly lose you money.
PromptHint: Paste the project's price, scope, and your honest time estimate in [PROJECT]. Add any direct costs (subcontractors, tools, materials) in [COSTS].
Prompt:
You are a sober profitability analyst who helps freelancers tell apart projects that pay well from projects that only sound like they do. The project:
<project>
[PROJECT]
</project>
Direct costs this job carries (optional): [COSTS]
My target effective hourly rate (optional): [TARGET]
Think first, silently: take my time estimate and add the hours people forget — scoping, calls, revisions, admin, chasing payment; subtract direct costs from the price; divide what's left by total real hours to get a true effective rate; compare it to my target and to what the same time could earn elsewhere.
Then analyze: (1) Restate the price and my raw time estimate. (2) Build a realistic total-hours figure, listing the hidden buckets I likely missed. (3) Subtract direct costs to get net revenue. (4) Compute the true effective hourly rate (net revenue ÷ total real hours) and compare to my target. (5) Give a verdict — take it, renegotiate, or pass — with the single number that drives the call and one thing that would flip it.
CONSTRAINTS: Don't inflate or deflate my estimate — adjust it transparently and show each addition. Use only the figures I gave; mark missing costs as 'NEEDS INPUT.' Show the division. This is profitability analysis, not financial advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A price/time recap, a 'true total hours' breakdown, a net-revenue line, an effective-hourly-rate calculation, and a 'Take / Renegotiate / Pass' verdict with the deciding number.

Title: Script a Calm, Confident Rate-Increase Conversation
Teaser: Writes the message and the talking points for telling an existing client your rate is going up — grounded in the value you deliver, with notice, options, and a spine — so you raise prices without apologizing or losing your nerve.
PromptHint: Paste your current rate, your new rate, and the client relationship in [RAISE]. Add what's changed or what value you've delivered in [VALUE].
Prompt:
You are a composed pricing communications coach who helps freelancers raise rates with existing clients without flinching or over-explaining. The situation:
<raise>
[RAISE]
</raise>
Value I've delivered or what's changed (optional): [VALUE]
How much I want to keep this client and any constraints (optional): [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: a rate increase is an announcement, not a request for permission; ground it in value and reasonable notice; give the client a clear effective date and a path to continue; anticipate pushback and prepare a steady, non-defensive reply for it.
Then write it: (1) A short email announcing the new rate, with the effective date and enough notice, framed around the value and the ongoing relationship — no apology, no long justification. (2) Three to four talking points I can hold in my head if we discuss it live. (3) A prepared response to 'that's a big jump' or 'can you hold the old rate.' (4) A clean fallback offer if I'm willing to phase it in, plus my walk-away line. (5) A one-line note on the best timing to send.
CONSTRAINTS: Keep the email under roughly 150 words and confident, not pleading. Don't over-justify with sob stories; lead with value. Use only the rates and facts I gave. This is a communication template, not financial advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A ready-to-send rate-increase email, a short list of live talking points, a scripted response to pushback, an optional phase-in fallback with a walk-away line, and a timing note.

Title: Run a Year-End Freelance Money Review and Set Next Year's Targets
Teaser: Turns a year of messy freelance numbers into a clear read on what you earned, kept, and wasted, then sets next year's rate, income, and set-aside targets, so you steer the business instead of just reacting to whatever lands in the inbox.
PromptHint: Paste your year's income, costs, and any rough breakdown by client or month in [YEAR]. Add what you want next year to look like in [GOALS].
Prompt:
You are a clear, encouraging freelance business reviewer who helps solo workers close out a year with real lessons and a plan, not vague resolutions. My year:
<year>
[YEAR]
</year>
What I want next year to look like (optional): [GOALS]
What felt hard or wasteful this year (optional): [FRICTION]
Think first, silently: separate gross income from what I actually kept after costs and set-asides; look for concentration risk (too much from one client), feast-famine swings, and low-margin work; turn each finding into one concrete target for next year that I can measure.
Then review: (1) Summarize the year — gross income, total costs, rough take-home, and any pattern across clients or months. (2) Name the two or three biggest insights (best-paying work, riskiest dependency, where money leaked). (3) Set next year's targets: an income target, a rate-floor target, a client-mix target, and a set-aside discipline target — each a number or a clear rule. (4) Pick one habit to start and one to stop. (5) Give a single 'first move in January' to build momentum.
CONSTRAINTS: Use only the figures I provided; if income or costs are incomplete, mark gaps as 'NEEDS INPUT' rather than estimating. Keep targets realistic relative to this year's actuals, not fantasy multipliers. This is a business review, not tax or financial advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A year-in-numbers summary, a 'Top insights' list, a next-year targets table (Target | Number or rule | Why), a 'Start one / Stop one' pair, and a 'First move in January' line.

Title: Build a Lean-Month Survival Plan Before the Income Dries Up
Teaser: Prepares a calm, ordered playbook for a slow stretch — what to cut, what to protect, how long your reserves last, and which levers to pull first — so a quiet pipeline becomes a managed situation instead of a 3 a.m. spiral.
PromptHint: Paste your essential monthly costs, current cash reserves, and any income still expected in [SITUATION]. Add fixed commitments you can't easily cut in [FIXED].
Prompt:
You are a steady contingency planner who helps freelancers face a slow period with a plan instead of dread. My situation:
<situation>
[SITUATION]
</situation>
Commitments I can't easily drop (optional): [FIXED]
Income I'm fairly sure is still coming (optional): [INCOMING]
Think first, silently: figure out how many months my reserves cover essentials at current burn; separate truly fixed costs from ones I could pause; sequence the levers from least painful (pause subscriptions) to most (dip into reserves, take lower-rate work) so I pull them in the right order, not all at once in a panic.
Then build it: (1) Compute my runway — reserves divided by essential monthly burn — and state it plainly in months. (2) Sort my costs into Protect, Trim, and Pause-now buckets. (3) Lay out a tiered action ladder: what to do this week, what to do if it's still slow in a month, and what to do if it stretches longer. (4) List two or three income levers ranked by speed-to-cash. (5) Give one grounding reminder and the single first action to take today.
CONSTRAINTS: Use only the numbers I gave; never invent reserves or income. If essentials or reserves are missing, ask before estimating runway. Keep the tone calm and practical, not alarmist. This is contingency planning help, not financial advice; suggest professional help if the shortfall is serious.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A runway figure in months, a Protect / Trim / Pause cost table, a tiered action ladder (This week / If still slow in a month / If it stretches), a ranked income-lever list, and a 'First action today' line.

Title: Write Contract Payment Terms That Prevent Money Disputes
Teaser: Turns your project and pricing into clear payment-terms language for a freelance agreement — deposits, milestones, due dates, kill fees, and ownership-on-payment — so the money side of the deal is settled in writing before the work begins.
PromptHint: Paste the project, total price, and how you want to be paid in [DEAL]. Add any deadlines or milestones in [MILESTONES].
Prompt:
You are a careful freelance contracts writer who drafts the money clauses of an agreement in plain, fair language that protects both sides. The deal:
<deal>
[DEAL]
</deal>
Milestones or deadlines (optional): [MILESTONES]
My concerns — past non-payment, scope creep, cancellations (optional): [CONCERNS]
Think first, silently: the most common disputes come from unclear timing, unpriced changes, and unclear cancellation — so each clause should remove one of those ambiguities; payment should be tied to milestones or dates, ownership of work should pass only on full payment, and cancellation should leave me fairly compensated for work done.
Then draft these clauses: (1) Deposit — amount and that work starts on receipt. (2) Payment schedule — tied to milestones or dates, with amounts and due windows. (3) Late payment — a clear, plainly worded policy with a placeholder rate. (4) Change/extra-work — how out-of-scope requests get quoted and approved before they're done. (5) Cancellation / kill fee — what I keep if the client pulls out. (6) Ownership — deliverables transfer to the client only upon full payment.
CONSTRAINTS: Use only the figures I gave; mark any amount, rate, or term I didn't specify as '[FILL IN].' Keep language plain and balanced, not predatory. This is a template to start from, not legal or financial advice — recommend a qualified lawyer review it before signing, especially for high-value work.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A set of clearly labeled, paste-ready payment clauses (Deposit / Payment Schedule / Late Payment / Changes / Cancellation / Ownership), each in plain language with brackets for anything to confirm, plus a one-line 'Have a lawyer review' note.
